app_subtitle: Browse the Ainu morpheme database — composition, valency, and how each morpheme combines.
search_placeholder: Type an Ainu word, e.g. si-nukar-e, yay-ko-nukar
empty_state_help: Search for a word above. If it's in the database, you'll see its lexical entry and any known internal structure. If it isn't, we'll try to segment it against the morpheme inventory and show a best-effort decomposition.
detail_empty: Click any node in the tree to inspect a morpheme's gloss, valency, and other appearances.
detail_slot_tooltip: Position in the Ainu verbal template (Bugaeva 2014, after Tamura 1955): I inner-applicative, II reflexive·reciprocal·indefinite-object, III outer-applicative, IV number, V direct causative / transitivising, VI indirect causative.
detail_observed: Observed {count} times in the Ainu corpora.
footer_credit: Curated entries follow the design in ainu-morpheme-database.
references_subtitle: Bibliography of dictionaries, corpora, and papers cited by the morpheme database.
morphemes_subtitle: Sortable table of every morpheme in the database. Click a lemma to inspect its composition tree.
lexemes_subtitle: Canonical, source-independent headwords. Each lexeme is composed of one or more morphemes; pivot one to see every dictionary's recording of it across dialects.
lexemes_detail_morphemes_hint: This lexeme's composition — open one in the morpheme explorer.
morphemes_filter_placeholder: Filter by lemma, gloss, or notes
detail_valency_inferred: Inferred from part of speech — no curated frame yet.
unseen_word_title: We haven't seen this word before
unseen_word_body: {word} isn't in the database, and we couldn't decompose it against the known morpheme inventory. Showing the input as-is.
composition_source_direct: Decomposition: not recorded — this is a single dictionary lemma
composition_source_composition: Decomposition: from the dictionary entry's recorded composition
composition_source_split: Decomposition: as you typed it (split on hyphens / whitespace) — not verified
composition_source_segmented: Decomposition: best-effort guess from known morphemes — not verified
processes_subtitle: Linguistic processes referenced by the morpheme database's etymology and composition entries.
processes_zero_deriv_body:
Ainu permits zero-derivation: a root can switch grammatical category without overt morphology. Sato 2020 documents this as the principal kind of category conversion in Ainu — verbs alternate with nouns, nouns with relational nouns, intransitive verbs with adverbial expressions, and so on. The typical pattern is an intransitive verb (arity +1) being re-used as an abstract noun root: the verbal argument disappears in the noun reading, so we annotate the operation as −1 on the etymology chain (the verb-form chip carries +1; the resulting noun-form chip has no verbal arity).

Examples:
• nu (vi, +1) 'sense / hear' → nu (n) 'eye' (cf. nu-pe 'eye-water = tear', nu-kar 'eye-act-on = see')
• itak (vi, +1) 'speak' → itak (n) 'word, speech, language'
• kar (vt, +2) 'act on, make' → kar (n) used in compounds as 'thing made, deed'

Reference: 佐藤2020アイヌ語における文法的カテゴリーの転換について — on the conversion of grammatical categories in Ainu.
processes_redup_body:
Ainu repeats a verb root to express iterative, distributive, or attenuated action. The reduplicated stem typically keeps the original argument structure (the +N badge does not change), but its meaning is intensified or pluractional. Reduplication is often followed by a causative suffix (-ke / -e), which adds a new causer slot.

Example:
• moy (vt, +2) 'move ~' → moymoy (vt, +2) 'move ~ repeatedly / fiddle with' → moymoyke (vt, +2) 'make-move-repeatedly = fiddle with, jiggle'

Reference: 佐藤2008アイヌ文法の基礎 §reduplication.
processes_affixation_body:
Most of the Ainu derivational machinery is overt affixation — productive prefixes and suffixes that modify a host's argument structure. These are shown directly in the synchronic Composition tree above the etymology frame; the etymology frame is reserved for analyses that are not transparent synchronically.

Productive prefixes covered by the database: i- (indefinite object), e- (applicative 'about/with'), ko- (applicative 'to/toward'), si- (indirect reflexive), yay- (direct reflexive), u- (reciprocal).

Productive suffixes: -e / -re / -te (causative, +1 causer), -yar / -ar (indefinite causative, arity-neutral), -pa (verbal plural).

Reference: ブガエワ2014アイヌ語使役構文に関する再考察 for the causative system; 佐藤2007アイヌ語千歳方言の再帰接頭辞yayとsiについて for the reflexive prefixes.
processes_grammaticalization_body:
A full lexical item is reanalysed as a more grammatical / functional element while preserving phonological form. The synchronic verb keeps its lexical sense in independent use, but inside compounds it functions as a light verb or grammatical operator. We tag this on the etymology chain when a compound-internal use diverges semantically from the free-standing form.

Example:
• kar (vt, +2) 'act on, do to ~' in compounds (nu-kar 'see' = 'eye-act-on'; e-kar-pe 'thing-made') ← grammaticalisation ← kar (vt, +2) 'make, create' as an independent verb. The compound-internal kar has bleached from 'make/create' to a generic 'act on ~' operator.

Reference: 佐藤2020アイヌ語における文法的カテゴリーの転換について for the broader phenomenon of category and meaning shift in Ainu.
